what_it_does: Provides a phased implementation guide for a graph redesign, including dependency order, principles, edit targets, cautions, pseudocode, and proof expectations for layout, colour, motion, interaction, substrate regeneration, and validation.
observations: Frames the work around augmenting the existing graph renderer instead of creating a parallel generator.
Defines a build DAG from foundations through proofs, with F1/F2/F3 as preconditions before renderer changes.
Separates derived substrate data, DNA token emission, graph layout, bind-layer motion, and proof work into named phases.
Uses repeated principle, sequence, files, and do-not-with-why sections for each subsystem.
Marks several decisions as OPEN→Tim gates, especially hue values, axis registers, file-node overview shape, mtime policy, dock scope, and cross-repo edge handling.
Includes pseudocode for local-coordinate node groups, grid placement, opacity scaling, and keyed SVG reconciliation.
standouts: The guide treats node group transforms as an enabling refactor for FLIP-style movement and zoom composition.
It assigns magnitude to opacity while preserving constant rings and count text.
It requires type hues to be resolved from DNA-facing data rather than hardcoded in renderer JavaScript.
It explicitly separates node persistence animation from camera easing.
conventions: Uses phase labels and requirement IDs such as F1, L1, M1, FV1, and PF-1 to organize work.
Declares file roles as NEW, MODIFY, REUSE, and REFERENCE.
Pairs most instructions with a stated principle and a why-oriented prohibition.
Uses uppercase emphasis for constraints, gates, and scope boundaries.
Includes line-number references and path-specific edit targets.
Uses project-local vocabulary such as substrate, DNA, face, registry-is-truth, derive-never-place, and one-focal.
concerns: Several values and behavioral choices remain gated by OPEN→Tim notes, so the guide is partly procedural and partly unresolved decision ledger.
Line-number references may become stale as target files change.
Some seed hue values are included while also being flagged as possibly unsuitable, which leaves implementers to resolve visual compliance later.
notes: This file is a detailed, constraint-heavy build guide for changing an existing graph instrument while preserving its architecture and validating behavior by use.
questions: Which type hue value set did Tim ratify for #G1?
Should the type hue face live in dna/tokens.json or dna/graph.json?
What axis register names and placements were chosen for #G2?
What shape was selected for overview file/content indication in #G5?
Was the cross-repo edge in registry/project.json implemented or backloged?
What proof harness commands correspond to the PF checks named here?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: it explains how to build to the graph redesign completion criteria through ordered phases, edit targets, pseudocode, and validation expectations.
intention_signals: It seems intended to guide an implementer through a constrained graph redesign while preventing architectural drift, hardcoded values, teleporting transitions, and visually ambiguous encodings.
novelty: The file combines implementation sequencing, design law language, dependency gating, and proof obligations in one guide rather than separating engineering tasks from visual-design rationale.
contribution: Contributes a detailed implementation ledger for graph redesign work: phase ordering, invariants, edit scopes, data-token requirements, motion behavior, visual encoding decisions, open gates, and proof expectations that a fused system would preserve as implementation intent and constraint context.
summary_for_embedding: A hardened implementation guide for the graph redesign that sequences substrate regeneration, DNA token work, renderer refactors, grid layout, type colour, edge treatment, keyed motion reconciliation, file visibility, and proof checks.
intention_for_embedding: This was written to make the graph redesign implementable without duplicating the existing renderer or violating project constraints around derivation, motion, colour, and validation.
